Study Title: Efficacy of tofacitinib in patients with rheumatoid arthritis stratified by baseline body mass index: an analysis of pooled data from phase 3 studies.
Authors: Dikranian AH, Gonzalez-Gay MA, Wellborne F, Alvaro-Gracia JM, Takiya L, Stockert L, Paulissen J, Shi H, Tatulych S, Curtis JR
Author Affiliations: Cabrillo Center for Rheumatic Disease, San Diego, California, USA.; Section of Rheumatology, Hospital Universitario Marques de Valdecilla, Santander, Spain.; Rheumatic Innovative Therapies, Houston Institute for Clinical Research, Houston, Texas, USA.; Rheumatology Department, Hospital General Universitario Gregorio Maranon, Madrid, Spain.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, Collegeville, Pennsylvania, USA.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, Collegeville, Pennsylvania, USA.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, New York, New York, USA.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, New York, New York, USA.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, Groton, Connecticut, USA.; Division of Clinical Immunology and Rheumatology, The University of Alabama at Birmingham, Birmingham, Alabama, USA jcurtis@uab.edu.
Key Finding: Baseline mean CRP levels were descriptive outcomes used to assess tofacitinib efficacy, showing that mean changes from baseline were greater for patients receiving tofacitinib versus placebo regardless of BMI category.
Key Finding Citation: Regardless of BMI category, mean changes from baseline (using descriptive analyses of observed data) at months 3 and 6 in DAS28-4(ESR), DAS28-4(CRP), CDAI, HAQ-DI and pain (VAS) were greater for patients receiving either tofacitinib dose versus placebo ( online supplemental figures 2 and 3 ).

Study Title: Efficacy of tofacitinib in patients with rheumatoid arthritis stratified by baseline body mass index: an analysis of pooled data from phase 3 studies.
Authors: Dikranian AH, Gonzalez-Gay MA, Wellborne F, Alvaro-Gracia JM, Takiya L, Stockert L, Paulissen J, Shi H, Tatulych S, Curtis JR
Author Affiliations: Cabrillo Center for Rheumatic Disease, San Diego, California, USA.; Section of Rheumatology, Hospital Universitario Marques de Valdecilla, Santander, Spain.; Rheumatic Innovative Therapies, Houston Institute for Clinical Research, Houston, Texas, USA.; Rheumatology Department, Hospital General Universitario Gregorio Maranon, Madrid, Spain.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, Collegeville, Pennsylvania, USA.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, Collegeville, Pennsylvania, USA.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, New York, New York, USA.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, New York, New York, USA.; Inflammation and Immunology, Pfizer Inc, Groton, Connecticut, USA.; Division of Clinical Immunology and Rheumatology, The University of Alabama at Birmingham, Birmingham, Alabama, USA jcurtis@uab.edu.
Key Finding: Obesity is characterized by low-grade systemic inflammation and is associated with increased production of proinflammatory cytokines, including TNF.
Key Finding Citation: Obesity is characterised by low-grade systemic inflammation, and is associated with increased production of proinflammatory cytokines, including TNF and interleukin-6, 21 and altered expression of adipokines, such as leptin, resistin and adiponectin.
